items.title: Stranger Strings: Yurei Ransomware Operator Toolkit Exposed
items.description: Active since September 2025, Yurei is a double extortion ransomware campaign. The operators run their own Tor data leak site with a low number of victims listed at the time of writing. It is reportedly derived from Prince Ransomware, an open-source ransomware family written in Go. Check Point researchers noted that all samples were first submitted to VirusTotal from Morocco, and that one sample di
items.ai_summary: Yurei ransomware is a double extortion campaign derived from Prince Ransomware, utilizing Go and exploiting public-facing applications. Its real-world impact includes data encryption and exfiltration, with a limited number of victims listed on its Tor data leak site. The most important defender action is to prioritize patching and securing public-facing applications to prevent initial exploitation.

    B -->|T1041| C[Exfiltration]:::tactic

    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Cisco Talos: Qilin EDR killer infection chain
items.description: Endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools are widely deployed and far more capable than traditional antivirus. As a result, attackers use EDR killers to disable or bypass them. The malicious “msimg32.dll” used in Qilin ransomware attacks, which is a multi-stage infection chain targeting EDR systems. It can terminate over 300 different EDR drivers from almost every vendor in the market.
items.ai_summary: The Qilin EDR killer is a multi-stage infection chain that targets endpoint detection and response systems, using a malicious 'msimg32.dll' to terminate over 300 EDR drivers. This allows attackers to bypass security measures and encrypt data for ransom. To defend against this threat, organizations should prioritize implementing robust EDR solutions and regularly updating their security software.

graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1104| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Blurred Lines: AdTech Abuse Delivers Browser Hijackers Through the Microsoft Store
items.description: A newly uncovered campaign abuses the Trillion (formerly Trellian) AdTech network, mimicking the flow of a Traffic Direction System (TDS) to trick visitors of typo-squatted domains into downloading Microsoft Store apps that contain browser hijacking malware. While the abuse of AdTech networks to deliver malware isn’t new, this campaign highlights incredibly similar tactics to VexTrio and previous
items.ai_summary: The threat involves abusing the Trillion AdTech network to deliver browser hijacking malware through Microsoft Store apps, leveraging typo-squatted domains to trick visitors. This campaign exhibits tactics similar to VexTrio, highlighting the evolving nature of AdTech abuse for malware distribution. Defenders should prioritize monitoring and filtering of suspicious Microsoft Store app downloads and traffic from typo-squatted domains.

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Signed malware impersonating workplace apps deploys RMM backdoors
items.description: Multiple phishing campaigns were identified using workplace meeting lures, PDF attachments, and abuse of legitimate binaries to deliver signed malware. The attacks used digitally signed executables masquerading as legitimate software to install remote monitoring and management (RMM) tools like ScreenConnect, Tactical RMM, and Mesh Agent. These tools enabled attackers to establish persistence and m
items.ai_summary: The threat involves signed malware masquerading as legitimate workplace software, delivered through phishing campaigns, to deploy RMM backdoors. This allows attackers to establish persistence and gain remote access. Defenders should prioritize verifying the authenticity of software and attachments to prevent initial access.

items.title: Securing the Supply Chain: How SentinelOne's AI EDR Stops the ...
items.description: On March 31, 2026, a North Korean state actor hijacked the npm credentials of the primary Axios maintainer and published two backdoored releases that deployed a cross-platform remote access trojan (RAT) to Windows, macOS, and Linux systems. Axios is the most widely used HTTP client in the JavaScript ecosystem, with approximately 100 million weekly downloads and a presence in roughly 80% of cloud a
items.ai_summary: A North Korean state actor compromised the npm credentials of the primary Axios maintainer, deploying a cross-platform remote access trojan (RAT) to Windows, macOS, and Linux systems. This supply chain attack leveraged the popularity of Axios, with approximately 100 million weekly downloads, to gain widespread access. To defend against this threat, organizations should immediately review and update their Axios dependencies to ensure they are not using the backdoored releases.

items.title: A Technique-Based Approach to Hunting Web-Delivered Malware
items.description: This report presents a technique-based approach to HTTP body hunting using Censys that addresses this tension directly, and demonstrates its effectiveness by walking through a live discovery: a ClickFix campaign delivering XWorm V5.6 through a 5-stage attack chain.
items.ai_summary: The threat involves a 5-stage attack chain delivering XWorm V5.6 malware through a ClickFix campaign, utilizing HTTP body hunting tactics. This technique-based approach highlights the importance of monitoring web-delivered malware. Defenders should prioritize implementing robust HTTP body inspection and vulnerability scanning detection to mitigate such threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1595.002| B[Discovery]:::tactic
    B -->|T1566| C[Initial Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic

    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: DPRK-Related Campaigns with LNK and GitHub C2
items.description: FortiGuard Labs recently detected a series of LNK files targeting users in South Korea. These attacks use a multi-stage scripting process and leverage GitHub as Command and Control (C2) infrastructure to evade detection. Although these LNK files can be traced back to 2024, earlier versions had less obfuscation and contained significant metadata, allowing us to track similar attacks spreading the X
items.ai_summary: The threat involves LNK files using a multi-stage scripting process to evade detection, with GitHub as Command and Control infrastructure. This campaign targets users in South Korea and has been ongoing since 2024. To defend against this threat, organizations should implement robust email and endpoint security measures to detect and block malicious LNK files.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

items.title: Iranian APT Infrastructure in Focus: Mapping State-Aligned Clusters During Geopolitical Escalation
items.description: The analysis examines Iranian state-aligned threat actors and their infrastructure patterns during heightened geopolitical tensions. It focuses on mapping network infrastructure, ASN patterns, TLS fingerprints, and hosting clusters associated with various Iranian APT groups. The report highlights the importance of proactive infrastructure monitoring to detect and disrupt potential cyber operations
items.ai_summary: Iranian state-aligned threat actors exploit heightened geopolitical tensions to map network infrastructure, steal authentication certificates, and gather victim host info. This proactive infrastructure monitoring is crucial to detect and disrupt potential cyber operations. Defenders must prioritize monitoring ASN patterns, TLS fingerprints, and hosting clusters.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1592| B[Gather Victim Host Info]:::tactic
    B -->|T1649| C[Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates]:::tactic
    C -->|T1566| D[Initial Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Execution]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: MuddyWater Exposed: Inside an Iranian APT operation
items.description: Researchers identified and analyzed exposed infrastructure of MuddyWater, an Iranian cyber espionage group linked to the Ministry of Intelligence and Security. The investigation revealed their reconnaissance methods, exploitation of vulnerabilities, custom command and control frameworks, and exfiltration techniques. Targets included organizations in Israel, Jordan, Egypt, UAE, Portugal, and the US
items.ai_summary: MuddyWater, an Iranian APT, uses reconnaissance, exploits vulnerabilities, and custom C2 frameworks to exfiltrate data from targets in multiple countries. Their tactics include Application Layer Protocol and Exfiltration Over C2 Channel. Defenders should monitor for suspicious network activity and implement vulnerability patching.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1071| B[Reconnaissance]:::tactic
    B -->|T1041| C[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: South American telecommunication providers targeted with three new malware implants
items.description: UAT-9244, a China-nexus advanced persistent threat actor, has been targeting critical telecommunications infrastructure in South America since 2024. The group employs three new malware implants: TernDoor, a Windows-based backdoor variant of CrowDoor; PeerTime, an ELF-based backdoor using BitTorrent protocol; and BruteEntry, a brute force scanner for SSH, Postgres, and Tomcat servers. UAT-9244 uses
items.ai_summary: UAT-9244, a China-nexus APT, targets South American telecoms with three new malware implants: TernDoor, PeerTime, and BruteEntry. These implants enable backdoor access, brute force attacks, and remote access software exploitation. Defenders should prioritize monitoring for T1190, T1110, and T1219 TTPs.

items.title: CVE-2026-4075: The BWL Advanced FAQ Manager Lite plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to Stored C...
items.description: The BWL Advanced FAQ Manager Lite plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to Stored Cross-Site Scripting via the 'baf_sbox' shortcode in all versions up to and including 1.1.1. This is due to insufficient input sanitization and output escaping on user-supplied shortcode attributes such as 'sbox_id', 'sbox_class', 'placeholder', 'highlight_color', 'highlight_bg', and 'cont_ext_class'. These attributes a
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-4075 is a Stored Cross-Site Scripting vulnerability in the BWL Advanced FAQ Manager Lite plugin for WordPress, allowing attackers to inject malicious code via user-supplied shortcode attributes. This vulnerability has a CVSS score of 6.4, indicating a medium severity risk. To defend against this threat, ensure the plugin is updated to a version that addresses this issue.

items.description: The Frontend Admin by DynamiApps plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to PHP Object Injection via deserialization of the 'post_content' of admin_form posts in all versions up to, and including, 3.28.31. This is due to the use of WordPress's `maybe_unserialize()` function without class restrictions on user-controllable content stored in admin_form post content. This makes it possible for authenticate
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-3328 is a PHP Object Injection vulnerability in the Frontend Admin plugin for WordPress, allowing authenticated attackers to exploit public-facing applications via deserialization of user-controllable content. This vulnerability affects all versions up to 3.28.31. Defenders should update the plugin to a patched version.

items.title: CVE-2026-4836: A vulnerability was detected in code-projects Accounting System 1.0. The affecte...
items.description: A vulnerability was detected in code-projects Accounting System 1.0. The affected element is an unknown function of the file /my_account/delete.php. Performing a manipulation of the argument cos_id results in sql injection. It is possible to initiate the attack remotely. The exploit is now public and may be used.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-4836 is a remote SQL injection vulnerability in code-projects Accounting System 1.0, allowing attackers to exploit public-facing applications. This vulnerability has a CVSS score of 6.3, indicating a medium severity impact. Defenders should update the Accounting System to the latest version to prevent exploitation.

items.title: CVE-2026-4835: A security vulnerability has been detected in code-projects Accounting System 1....
items.description: A security vulnerability has been detected in code-projects Accounting System 1.0. Impacted is an unknown function of the file /my_account/add_costumer.php of the component Web Application Interface. Such manipulation of the argument costumer_name leads to cross site scripting. The attack may be performed from remote. The exploit has been disclosed publicly and may be used.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-4835 is a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability in code-projects Accounting System 1.0, exploitable from remote, and publicly disclosed. This vulnerability allows an attacker to manipulate the costumer_name argument in /my_account/add_costumer.php. Defenders should update the Accounting System to the latest version to mitigate this vulnerability.

items.title: CVE-2014-125112: Plack::Middleware::Session::Cookie versions through 0.21 for Perl allows remote ...
items.description:
Plack::Middleware::Session::Cookie versions through 0.21 for Perl allows remote code execution.

Plack::Middleware::Session::Cookie versions through 0.21 has a security vulnerability where it allows an attacker to execute arbitrary code on the server during deserialization of the cookie data, when there is no secret used to sign the cookie.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: ‘Starkiller’ Phishing Service Proxies Real Login Pages, MFA
items.description: Most phishing websites are little more than static copies of login pages for popular online destinations, and they are often quickly taken down by anti-abuse activists and security firms. But a stealthy new phishing-as-a-service offering lets customers sidestep both of these pitfalls: It uses cleverly disguised links to load the target brand's real website, and then acts as a relay between the target and the legitimate site -- forwarding the victim's username, password and multi-factor authentication (MFA) code to the legitimate site and returning its responses.

items.title: Kimwolf Botnet Swamps Anonymity Network I2P
items.description: For the past week, the massive "Internet of Things" (IoT) botnet known as Kimwolf has been disrupting the The Invisible Internet Project (I2P), a decentralized, encrypted communications network designed to anonymize and secure online communications. I2P users started reporting disruptions in the network around the same time the Kimwolf botmasters began relying on it to evade takedown attempts against the botnet's control servers.
